title: The page was never bare
checkLabels.t4: The margin around the body is gone
why.empty: The editor is empty. You deleted the file instead of adding rules. Bring it back and start again.
why.untouched: The code is exactly as it was. You haven't taken anything off yet.
hint2.untouched: You write in the `styles.css` tab. `index.html` isn't touched in this lesson.
why.not-closed: A tag is open and never closed.
hint2.not-closed: If you've edited `index.html`, restore it. The work is only in `styles.css`.
why.unlinked: The page can't find `styles.css` any more. You've edited `index.html`.
hint2.unlinked: Restore both files and work only in `styles.css`.
why.no-body: There is no body. You've edited `index.html`.
why.no-h1: There is no heading. You've edited `index.html` — the work is in the other file.
why.no-ul: There is no list. You've edited `index.html`.
why.no-a: There is no link. You've edited `index.html`.
why.body-margin: The text still doesn't reach the edge. There's a margin you never wrote — it comes from the browser. The check looks at the top and the left.
hint2.body-margin: The margin is around the whole body of the page, on all four sides. So the rule is for `body`, and one value covers all of them.
hint3.body-margin: Eight pixels, on all four sides. The number is in the W3C sample stylesheet from 1998 and has been in almost every browser since. That's why the first rule in almost every real site removes it.
hint4.body-margin: Give `body` a `margin` of `0`. One number covers all four sides.
why.h1-still-big: The heading is still larger than the text. Nobody wrote that it should be — the browser decided.
hint2.h1-still-big: The paragraph is 16 pixels. Make the heading the same and see what's left of it.
hint3.h1-still-big: A heading defaults to `2em` — twice the text size, so 32 pixels. Not because `h1` means large, but because that number sits in the W3C sample stylesheet from 1998 and every browser took it from there.
why.bullets-left: The list items still have discs in front of them. You didn't put those there either.
hint2.bullets-left: The property is called `list-style-type`. Look for a value that means no marker.
hint3.bullets-left: Every navigation menu on the web is a `ul`. Not one of them has discs — because every one removed them exactly like this.
hint4.bullets-left: In a rule for `ul` set `list-style-type: none`.
why.underline-left: The link is still underlined. That's the browser's decision too, not yours.
hint3.underline-left: Careful here. An underline on a link isn't decoration — it's the only thing that tells a link apart from text for someone who can't tell the colours apart. Take it off and you owe them another signal.
hint4.underline-left: In a rule for `a` set `text-decoration: none`. And remember what you lost.
why.lost-li: A list item is gone. The work is in `styles.css`.
blocks.1.text: The cafe is opening a second page — the menu. It's still bare: `index.html` is given, and `styles.css` holds exactly one rule, the font.
blocks.2.text: And still: the heading is large, the list has discs, the link is blue and underlined, everything sits away from the edge. In lesson 01 I wrote that this is what a page without CSS looks like. That wasn't true, and now you'll prove it yourself.
blocks.4.text: Every browser carries its own stylesheet and applies it before yours ever arrives. It decides that:
blocks.5.items.0: `h1` is `2em` — twice the size of the text
blocks.5.items.3: `body` has 8 pixels of margin on all four sides
blocks.6.text: Those numbers weren't invented by the browser makers. They sit in the sample stylesheet attached to the CSS2 specification from 1998, and they were copied from there. That's why they're identical in browsers that agree on nothing else.
blocks.8.text: So that a page whose author wrote no styles is still readable. Open a scientific document from 1998 — it has headings, paragraphs, lists. It works. That's insurance, not aesthetics.
blocks.9.text: And something important follows: you don't start from nothing. You start from someone else's decision you've never seen, and sometimes your first job is to take it off. That's why real sites open with a few lines that remove the defaults.
blocks.10.text: There are two approaches and they aren't the same thing. A *reset* strips everything and returns the elements to a bare start. A *normalize* keeps what's useful and only fixes the differences between browsers. What you're about to write is a reset — a small one, four rules.
blocks.11.text: And one more thing: someone else's decision changes. Until 2025 an `h1` inside a `section` or an `article` was drawn smaller, like an `h2`. That rule has been removed from the spec and browsers are dropping it right now. Which is why MDN says to set your own heading sizes instead of relying on theirs.
blocks.13.text: In `styles.css` take off four things: the margin around the body, the large heading, the discs on the list, and the underline on the link. Don't touch `index.html`. About that underline — know that you're doing something arguable. We'll settle the bill one lesson from now.
blocks.16.items.0: A page with no CSS of yours isn't a page with no CSS. It carries the browser's stylesheet.
blocks.16.items.1: The numbers come from the W3C sample stylesheet of 1998: `body` 8px, `h1` 2em.
blocks.16.items.2: A reset strips everything. A normalize keeps what's useful and levels the browsers. Not synonyms.
blocks.16.items.3: Defaults change over the years. Whatever you actually need — set it yourself.
blocks.17.text: Look at the preview. That's a page truly without styles. It's ugly in a different way, and now you know exactly why.
walkthrough.0.text: `margin: 0` on `body` — one value covers all four sides. The check looks at the top and the left on purpose: `margin-top: 0` looks like the job is done while the text still sits eight pixels from the left edge.
walkthrough.1.text: `font-size: 16px` on `h1`. `1rem` works too — the same number expressed against the root size, and the check accepts it. The heading stays bold, because we never touched `font-weight`. Large and bold are two separate decisions by the browser and they come off separately.
walkthrough.2.text: `list-style-type: none` removes the discs but NOT the indent on the left. That's `padding` on the `ul` itself and it stays. The list looks shifted for no reason — your first meeting with something you'll meet until the end: properties don't do what their name promises.
walkthrough.3.text: `text-decoration: none` on `a`. I wrote the shorthand, while the check asks for `text-decoration-line` — they're the same thing, the long name is just what the browser gives back when you ask it. For the shorthand it returns three values at once and the comparison stops being reliable.
